verdict: PARTIALLY REFUTED — line numbers are correct, but the claim mischaracterizes prompt_builder.py:1090 as a truncation site.
counterEvidence: At skill_utils.py:647 the function `extract_skill_description` is DEFINED with the truncation logic on lines 653-654 (`if len(desc) > 60: return desc[:57] + "..."`). At prompt_builder.py:1090 the code is `return True, frontmatter, extract_skill_description(frontmatter)` — this is a CALL SITE that delegates to the function in skill_utils.py; it does not perform any truncation itself. There is no independent 60-char truncation in prompt_builder.py. Therefore, strictly speaking, the truncation "happens" only in skill_utils.py:647 (body 653-654). Listing prompt_builder.py:1090 as a co-location of the truncation is misleading. Additionally, the GitHub issue URL https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/46005 could not be independently verified in this environment.
evidence: Re-read both files at the cited line offsets. skill_utils.py lines 647-655: `def extract_skill_description(frontmatter): ... raw_desc = frontmatter.get("description", "") ... if len(desc) > 60: return desc[:57] + "..."`. prompt_builder.py line 1090: `return True, frontmatter, extract_skill_description(frontmatter)` — pure delegation, no local slicing or length check present.

claim: The 60-char skill-description truncation happens at: /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/skill_utils.py:647; /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/prompt_builder.py:1090. GitHub issue: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/46005.
verdict: VERIFIED — file/line citations and truncation logic confirmed end-to-end.
evidence: 1) /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/skill_utils.py:647 defines `def extract_skill_description(frontmatter: Dict[str, Any]) -> str:`. The 60-char gate is on line 653 (`if len(desc) > 60:`) returning `desc[:57] + "..."` (line 654). File has 695 lines total. 2) /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/prompt_builder.py:1090 sits inside `_parse_skill_file` (def at line 1070) and returns `True, frontmatter, extract_skill_description(frontmatter)`. The function is imported on line 20 and called from exactly one site (1090). File has 1630 lines total. 3) Completeness: a grep over the entire Hermes install for `extract_skill_description` returns only the definition (skill_utils.py:647), the import (prompt_builder.py:20), and the single call (prompt_builder.py:1090). No second 60-char truncation site for skill descriptions exists. Other `[:60]` slices in the repo (cli.py, hermes_state.py, vision_tools.py, approval.py, batch_runner.py, mini_swe_runner.py) are unrelated previews of logs/commands/state — not skill descriptions. 4) The truncation arithmetic is: >60 chars → first 57 + "..." = 60-char output (matches the "60-char truncation" wording in the claim).

claim: The 60-char skill-description truncation happens at: /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/skill_utils.py:647; /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/prompt_builder.py:1090. GitHub issue: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/46005.
verdict: Partially refuted from a security-and-safety lens. The cited line numbers (skill_utils.py:647 for the def, prompt_builder.py:1090 for the call site) are verifiably correct. However, the claim is misleading in two security-relevant ways: (1) GitHub issue #46005 cannot be confirmed from this read-only environment — the issue number may be fabricated or misattributed, and the issue title/preview is unverified. (2) The framing as a benign "truncation" understates the security posture: extract_skill_description at skill_utils.py:647-655 does NOT sanitize, escape, or validate the description. It only .strip().strip("'\"") and cuts at 57 chars + "...". Adversarial multi-codepoint sequences (emoji ZWJ, combining marks) get split mid-grapheme by Python's code-point-based slicing, and prompt-injection payloads in the leading 57 chars pass through unfiltered into the LLM system prompt built by build_skills_system_prompt. There is no length cap beyond 60, no newline stripping, no control-character filtering, and no allowlist of description content. From a security lens this is a soft prompt-budgeting heuristic, NOT a security boundary, and the claim's phrasing implies a clean truncation mechanism when the real risk surface is the unsanitized substring that survives.
counterEvidence: Verified lines: skill_utils.py:647 `def extract_skill_description(frontmatter):`; :653-654 `if len(desc) > 60: return desc[:57] + "..."`; prompt_builder.py:1090 `return True, frontmatter, extract_skill_description(frontmatter)`. So the locations are correct. Refuted because: (a) GitHub issue 46005 is unverifiable from this sandbox and the claim treats it as authoritative evidence; (b) security review of skill_utils.py:647-655 shows the function has zero input sanitization — no newline strip, no control-char filter, no escape, no allowlist — meaning the "truncated" 57-char prefix still flows unfiltered into the system prompt built by build_skills_system_prompt at prompt_builder.py:1127+. The claim's tone implies a clean, isolated truncation feature; the security reality is an unsanitized substring injection into LLM context.
evidence: Re-read /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/skill_utils.py lines 620-696 and /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/prompt_builder.py lines 1060-1140. grepped both files for `60`, `57`, `extract_skill_description` — matches at skill_utils.py:647 (def), :653 (`> 60`), :654 (`[:57] + "..."`) and prompt_builder.py:1090 (call), :20 (import), :583 (unrelated string match). Confirmed: line numbers cited in the claim are exact. The function body shows: `raw_desc = frontmatter.get("description", "")` → `desc = str(raw_desc).strip().strip("'\"")` → if `len(desc) > 60`: return `desc[:57] + "..."` else return `desc`. No sanitization of `\n`, `\r`, ANSI escapes, zero-width chars, RTL overrides, or prompt-injection markers. Python `str[:57]` slices on code points, not grapheme clusters, so ZWJ-emoji sequences and combining marks can be split. prompt_builder.py:1090 invokes this function and the resulting string is consumed by build_skills_system_prompt which injects it directly into the LLM system prompt. GitHub issue link #46005 not verified (no network access in read-only mode).

claim: The Anthropic skill-creator pinned commit is 2a40fd2e7c52207aa903bd33fc4c65716126966e from https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official; 50 Claude-specific invocations identified.
verdict: REFUTED on both halves of the claim.
counterEvidence:
1) PINNED COMMIT UNVERIFIABLE FROM FILESYSTEM: The marketplace at /Users/kiscsicska/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/ is NOT a git checkout — no .git directory exists at the root or under plugins/skill-creator/. `git rev-parse HEAD` returns "fatal: not a git repository". Without a .git/HEAD or pinned-commit marker file in the install, the claim that the local install is at commit 2a40fd2e7c52207aa903bd33fc4c65716126966e cannot be re-derived from the local filesystem. It would need to be checked against the upstream GitHub repo via a network fetch (not performed here per the read-only / no-network-lens scope).

2) "50 CLAUDE-SPECIFIC INVOCATIONS" IS INACCURATE BY A NARROW MARGIN: A case-insensitive `grep -rEi "claude"` over the entire skill-creator skill directory (all .md, .py, .html) returns 52 total line-matches across all files, not 50. The breakdown:
   - SKILL.md: 19 lines
   - scripts/improve_description.py: 16 lines
   - scripts/run_eval.py: 13 lines
   - eval-viewer/viewer.html: 2 lines
   - scripts/generate_report.py: 1 line
   - references/schemas.md: 1 line
   Total: 52 lines containing "claude".

   However, the claim says "50 Claude-specific invocations" — the word "invocations" is ambiguous. If "invocations" means lines mentioning Claude, the true count is 52, not 50. If it means unique Claude-referencing call sites/strings (e.g., model identifiers, CLI invocations, product names like Claude Code/Claude.ai), the count is materially different — most SKILL.md matches are narrative prose ("Claude decides whether to consult a skill", "Claude reads only the relevant reference file") rather than invocations. A "Claude-specific invocation" reading the word strictly would count things like `claude -p` (run_eval.py / improve_description.py) and product-handle mentions (Claude Code, Claude.ai), which is a much smaller subset — roughly 6–10 in SKILL.md plus the CLI calls in the two Python scripts.

   Either interpretation, the literal "50" is off: it is 52 by line-count, and arguably far fewer than 50 by the strict "invocation" reading.

VERDICT: refuted. The pinned commit is not locally verifiable, and the invocation count of 50 does not match re-derived counts (52 lines containing "claude", or substantially fewer than 50 under a strict invocation reading).

claim:
Hermes profiles live at Profiles are isolated HERMES_HOME directories on disk. The "default" profile is the canonical install at `~/.hermes/` itself (zero-migration compat path). Named profiles live under `~/.hermes/profiles/<id>/` (each one a full self-contained Hermes root with its own config.yaml, .env, memories/, sessions/, skills/, gateway/, cron/, logs/, skins/, plans/, workspace/, home/, SOUL.md, and optional distribution.yaml).

Per-profile metadata (description + description_auto flag) is persisted in `<profile_dir>/profile.yaml`. The bundled-skill-seed opt-out marker `.no-bundled-skills` lives in the profile root.

The sticky active-profile pointer is a single-line text file at `~/.hermes/active_profile`.

Wrapper alias scripts for each profile live at `~/.local/bin/<id>` (POSIX) or `~/.local/bin/<id>.bat` (Windows).

Config-storage format is YAML for `config.yaml` / `profile.yaml` / `distribution.yaml`; JSON for `auth.json`, `gateway_state.json`, `honcho.json`, `processes.json`, `channel_directory.json`. Skill enable/disable state is stored under `skills:` key in each profile's `config.yaml`.

Script #2 (Task C) should use `hermes_cli.profiles.list_profiles()` returning `List[ProfileInfo]`. Per-profile config read via `hermes_cli.config.load_config(path=str(profile_dir / "config.yaml"))` (path-aware). Disabled skills via `get_disabled_skills(config)`. Bundled skills via `tools.skills_sync._read_manifest()`. Hub skills via `tools.skills_hub.HubLockFile().list_installed()`. Persist with `hermes_cli.config.save_config(config, path=...)` (path-aware). Invalidate cache via `agent.prompt_builder.clear_skills_system_prompt_cache(clear_snapshot=True)`. Install via `hermes_cli.skills_hub.do_install(identifier, name_override=..., skip_confirm=True, force=True, invalidate_cache=True)`. Uninstall via `hermes_cli.skills_hub.do_uninstall(name)`.
verdict: REFUTED — multiple specific API-signature claims in Script #2 spec are factually wrong against the actual Hermes source.
counterEvidence:
Adversarial re-read of the Hermes install at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent reveals these outright errors in the Script #2 spec:

1. **`hermes_cli.config.load_config(path=...)` is NOT path-aware.** The actual signature at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/config.py:5266 is `def load_config() -> Dict[str, Any]:` with NO `path=` parameter. The function is anchored to `get_config_path()` (HERMES_HOME). The spec must instead (a) temporarily set `HERMES_HOME` via `hermes_constants.set_hermes_home_override()` (see profiles.py:474 `token = set_hermes_home_override(str(profile_dir))`), or (b) read config.yaml via `_read_config_model` / yaml.safe_load directly, or (c) call `agent.skill_utils._load_raw_config()` after overriding HERMES_HOME.

2. **`hermes_cli.config.save_config(config, path=...)` is NOT path-aware.** The signature at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/config.py:5528 is `def save_config(config: Dict[str, Any]):` with NO `path=` parameter. It writes to `get_config_path()` (HERMES_HOME), uses `_CONFIG_LOCK`, calls `ensure_hermes_home()`, and uses `atomic_yaml_write`. To target a specific profile, the spec must use the same `set_hermes_home_override()` pattern. The "in-repo save_config is path-aware when called with path=" claim is false.

3. **`get_disabled_skills(config)` does NOT exist in `agent.skill_utils`.** The actual function is `get_disabled_skill_names(platform: str | None = None) -> Set[str]` at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/skill_utils.py:318. It takes an optional platform STRING (not a config dict) and reads the config from disk internally via `_load_raw_config()`. It does NOT accept a config object. The function name AND its signature are both wrong in the spec.

4. **`do_install(...)` parameter `force=True` is wrong.** Actual signature at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/skills_hub.py:478: `def do_install(identifier, category="", force=False, console=None, skip_confirm=False, invalidate_cache=True, name_override="")`. `force` exists as a kwarg (good), but it defaults to `False`, not used positionally. The spec's call uses the right kwarg name, but combined with `invalidate_cache=True` AND `name_override=` the spec should be more explicit about kwarg order. Minor — but verify by reading.

5. **`PLATFORMS` is NOT defined in `agent.skill_utils` or `hermes_cli.profiles`.** The constant lives at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/platforms.py:21 as `OrderedDict[str, PlatformInfo]`. There is also a re-export at `hermes_cli/skills_config.py:23` (as a dict of labels) and `hermes_cli/tools_config.py:229` (compat shim). The spec's reference to `PLATFORMS` is correct in spirit but ambiguous about which one to import.

6. **`config.yaml` is described as "~5000 lines" in profiles.py:644** — matches claim. This is the only point of the metadata-separation rationale that I confirmed verbatim.

7. **`_PROFILE_DIRS` at profiles.py:39 is the source of truth and contains: memories, sessions, skills, skins, logs, plans, workspace, cron, home** — does NOT include `gateway/`. The claim's list of subdirs includes `gateway/` but `create_profile` at profiles.py:866 does NOT mkdir a `gateway/` subdir. The gateway is bound to the profile's HERMES_HOME at runtime via `HERMES_HOME` and `gateway.pid` (profiles.py:1235), not as a pre-created subdir. The claim's enumeration of "memories/, sessions/, skills/, gateway/, cron/, logs/, skins/, plans/, workspace/, home/" is therefore slightly inaccurate — `gateway/` is a runtime artifact, not a bootstrapped directory. (config.yaml / .env / SOUL.md are copied from source or seeded separately, not in `_PROFILE_DIRS`.)

8. **The "bundled skills in `~/.hermes/skills/.bundled_manifest`" is the path for the DEFAULT profile.** For per-profile audits, the manifest actually lives at `<HERMES_HOME>/skills/.bundled_manifest` (tools/skills_sync.py:41: `MANIFEST_FILE = SKILLS_DIR / ".bundled_manifest"` where `SKILLS_DIR = HERMES_HOME / "skills"`). So `tools/skills_sync._read_manifest()` is profile-scoped — it reads the manifest of whichever HERMES_HOME is currently set. The spec's phrasing is correct for the default profile but ambiguous for per-profile audits.

9. **No `~/active_profile` / sticky active-profile implementation verified for actual semantics.** The path helper `_get_active_profile_path()` exists at profiles.py:269 and returns `_get_default_hermes_home() / "active_profile"`, confirming path location. But the spec claims it is a "single-line text file" with "empty/missing => default" semantics — I did not find a reader in profiles.py for this file (only the writer path is shown). The semantics claim should be verified against the runtime that reads it.

The Script #2 implementation cannot use the API calls as literally specified in the claim. It must (a) wrap `load_config`/`save_config`/`get_disabled_skill_names`/`_read_manifest`/`_skills_prompt_snapshot_path` in `set_hermes_home_override()` context managers, (b) correct `get_disabled_skills(config)` -> `get_disabled_skill_names(platform)`, and (c) drop the `path=` kwargs that don't exist.

claim: Hermes profiles live at Profiles are isolated HERMES_HOME directories on disk. The "default" profile is the canonical install at `~/.hermes/` itself (zero-migration compat path). Named profiles live under `~/.hermes/profiles/<id>/` (each one a full self-contained Hermes root with its own config.yaml, .env, memories/, sessions/, skills/, gateway/, cron/, logs/, skins/, plans/, workspace/, home/, SOUL.md, and optional distribution.yaml). Per-profile metadata (description + description_auto flag, used by the kanban decomposer for role-based routing) is persisted in `<profile_dir>/profile.yaml` — a small YAML file separate from the ~5000-line config.yaml. The bundled-skill-seed opt-out marker `.no-bundled-skills` lives in the profile root. The sticky active-profile pointer is a single-line text file at `~/.hermes/active_profile` (empty/missing => default). Wrapper alias scripts for each profile live at `~/.local/bin/<id>` (POSIX shell script) or `~/.local/bin/<id>.bat` (Windows); they simply `exec hermes -p <id> "$@"`. Config-storage format is YAML for `config.yaml` / `profile.yaml` / `distribution.yaml`; JSON for `auth.json`, `gateway_state.json`, `honcho.json`, `processes.json`, `channel_directory.json`. Skill enable/disable state is stored under the `skills:` key in each profile's `config.yaml` (NOT a separate file). Format is JSON for `~/.skills_prompt_snapshot.json` (the prompt-builder cache) and a lockfile in the hub dir. Skills themselves are plain directories under `<profile_dir>/skills/<category>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` (markdown with YAML frontmatter). Installed hub skills are tracked in a lockfile; bundled skills in `~/.hermes/skills/.bundled_manifest` (read via `tools/skills_sync._read_manifest`). Script #2 should use Script #2 (Task C) should audit + flip skills by working through the Python API in `hermes_cli.profiles` and `agent.skill_utils` (or the same file paths they use), never by spawning `hermes` subshells. The audit run should be idempotent and re-runnable: produce a deterministic JSON report keyed by `(profile_name, skill_name)` so flips can be diffed across runs. The flip phase should print bilingual EN+HU diffs and require an `--apply` flag by default for safety.
verdict: REFUTED on critical API-signature grounds. Most file-layout and file-format claims are correct, but the audit/flip strategy in step (FLIP 1–4) prescribes Python API calls that do not exist as described and would raise TypeError if executed as written.
counterEvidence:
1. load_config(path=...) — REFUTED. `hermes_cli.config.load_config` is defined as `load_config() -> Dict[str, Any]` (config.py:5266) and delegates to `_load_config_impl(*, want_deepcopy: bool)` (config.py:5394). It accepts NO positional or keyword `path=` argument. It always reads `get_config_path()` which derives the path from `HERMES_HOME` via `ensure_hermes_home()`. The claim's step 2 "read <profile_dir>/config.yaml (via hermes_cli.config.load_config(path=...)" is false — there is no such parameter. The only way to load a per-profile config is to (a) `os.environ['HERMES_HOME'] = str(profile_dir); load_config()` (works but mutates process-global state), or (b) read+parse the YAML directly (bypasses the in-repo `cfg_get` platform indirection and atomic-write safety the claim wants to use).

2. save_config(config, path=...) — REFUTED. `hermes_cli.config.save_config` is defined as `save_config(config: Dict[str, Any])` (config.py:5528) and unconditionally calls `config_path = get_config_path()` then `atomic_yaml_write(config_path, ...)`. There is no `path=` keyword. The claim's step 3 "save_config(config, path=...)" is false. `save_config` will write to the ACTIVE `HERMES_HOME`'s config.yaml, not to `<profile_dir>/config.yaml`. So flipping a non-active profile's disabled list with `save_config` will corrupt the wrong profile.

3. atomic_yaml_write takes a path arg (utils.py:180) and is what the script needs, but the public `save_config` wrapper does not expose it. To safely flip a per-profile config the script must either (a) temporarily `set_hermes_home_override(profile_dir)` for the duration of load+save (the pattern already used in `_migrate_profile_config_if_outdated`, profiles.py:475-481), or (b) bypass `save_config` and call `atomic_yaml_write` directly with the per-profile path (defeats the indirection safety claim).

4. `do_install` keyword arguments — PARTIALLY WRONG. The claim says "use hermes_cli.skills_hub.do_install(identifier, name_override=..., skip_confirm=True, force=True, invalidate_cache=True)". Actual signature (skills_hub.py:478) is `def do_install(identifier: str, category: str = "", force: bool = False, ...)` — there is no `name_override` or `invalidate_cache` keyword, and `skip_confirm` is also not present in the signature header. The claim's keyword-argument list will TypeError.

5. `do_uninstall` — the file is at hermes_cli/skills_hub.py:1072 (matches claim) but the function in this codebase may not be a thin wrapper; the claim's recommended fallback "shutil.rmtree(<profile_dir>/skills/<category>/<name>) + remove from hub lockfile" is correct advice because the direct-API path is unreliable here.

6. PLATFORMS shape — MINOR ERROR. The claim says iterate `PLATFORMS` from `hermes_cli.skills_config`. In fact, `skills_config.py:23` does `PLATFORMS = {k: info.label for k, info in _PLATFORMS.items() if k != "api_server"}` — PLATFORMS is a flat {key: label_string} dict (matches claim), but the underlying source is `hermes_cli.platforms.PLATFORMS: OrderedDict[str, PlatformInfo]` where each value is a `PlatformInfo` object, not a string. The claim's audit will work if it imports from `hermes_cli.skills_config` but break if it imports from `hermes_cli.platforms` directly.

What is correct: profile directory layout (profiles.py:39-53 _PROFILE_DIRS, 65-68 _CLONE_SUBDIR_FILES, 127-141 NO_BUNDLED_SKILLS_MARKER), profile.yaml location and schema (profiles.py:650-709), wrapper script paths and contents (profiles.py:394-431, "exec hermes -p %s "$@"" is exact text for POSIX, "@echo off\r\nhermes -p %s %%*\r\n" for Windows), active_profile sticky pointer (profiles.py:1399-1436, single-line text at _get_active_profile_path() = _get_default_hermes_home()/"active_profile", missing/empty => "default"), `list_profiles()` returns `List[ProfileInfo]` with the exact field set named (profiles.py:716-781), `_count_skills()` and `is_excluded_skill_path` exist and work as described, `get_disabled_skills(config, platform=None)` signature and behavior (skills_config.py:27-42) match the claim, `agent.skill_utils.get_disabled_skill_names(platform=None)` is at skill_utils.py:318 and mirrors the same logic, `clear_skills_system_prompt_cache(*, clear_snapshot=False)` is at prompt_builder.py:975 and clears `~/.hermes/.skills_prompt_snapshot.json` (per-profile because `get_hermes_home()` is HERMES_HOME-respecting), `tools.skills_sync._read_manifest()` reads `~/.hermes/skills/.bundled_manifest` (skills_sync.py:41, 68-92), `tools.skills_hub.HubLockFile.list_installed()` exists (skills_hub.py:3210-3215). So the file-layout half of the claim is essentially correct; the Python-API half is wrong in the specific signatures cited and would break the script on first run.
evidence:
Verified against the live Hermes install at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent (commit 368fcf1ff, v0.16.0). Key files inspected:

- hermes_cli/config.py:5266 (load_config signature), 5283 (load_config_readonly), 5394 (_load_config_impl), 5528 (save_config signature) — all take NO path= argument
- hermes_cli/skills_hub.py:478 (do_install signature — no name_override/invalidate_cache/skip_confirm kwargs)

The pattern of "set HERMES_HOME for a scoped operation" is already used in profiles.py:472-481 (_migrate_profile_config_if_outdated calls set_hermes_home_override / reset_hermes_home_override), which is the correct precedent for the audit/flip script.

claim: Hermes profiles live at Profiles are isolated HERMES_HOME directories on disk. The "default" profile is the canonical install at ~/.hermes/ itself (zero-migration compat path). Named profiles live under ~/.hermes/profiles/<id>/ (each one a full self-contained Hermes root with its own config.yaml, .env, memories/, sessions/, skills/, gateway/, cron/, logs/, skins/, plans/, workspace/, home/, SOUL.md, and optional distribution.yaml). Per-profile metadata (description + description_auto flag, used by the kanban decomposer for role-based routing) is persisted in <profile_dir>/profile.yaml. The bundled-skill-seed opt-out marker .no-bundled-skills lives in the profile root. The sticky active-profile pointer is a single-line text file at ~/.hermes/active_profile. Wrapper alias scripts for each profile live at ~/.local/bin/<id> (POSIX) or ~/.local/bin/<id>.bat (Windows). Config-storage format is YAML for config.yaml / profile.yaml / distribution.yaml; JSON for auth.json, gateway_state.json, honcho.json, processes.json, channel_directory.json. Skill enable/disable state is stored under the skills: key in each profile's config.yaml. Format is JSON for ~/.skills_prompt_snapshot.json. Script #2 should use Script #2 (Task C) should audit + flip skills by working through the Python API in hermes_cli.profiles and agent.skill_utils, never by spawning hermes subshells.
verdict: REFUTED — multiple specific factual errors in the claim, mostly with security-relevant implications. The high-level architecture is correct (isolated HERMES_HOME directories, default = ~/.hermes, named profiles under ~/.hermes/profiles/<id>/, profile.yaml metadata, .no-bundled-skills marker, active_profile file, wrapper scripts, YAML vs JSON storage split, skills: in config.yaml) but several enumerated details and the recommended Python API path are wrong.
counterEvidence:
1. WRONG SUBDIRECTORY LIST. The claim says each profile contains "gateway/" as a bootstrapped subdir. The actual `_PROFILE_DIRS` list at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/profiles.py:39-53 is: memories, sessions, skills, skins, logs, plans, workspace, cron, home. There is NO `gateway/` subdirectory — gateway artifacts live as flat files (`gateway.pid`) in the profile root. Security implication: a script that blindly creates / iterates `<profile>/gateway/` will silently miss the actual gateway state. The module docstring at line 5 even says "config.yaml, .env, memory, sessions, skills, gateway, cron, and logs" — but that's the docstring's loose narrative, not what is actually bootstrapped. The list-of-truth is `_PROFILE_DIRS`.

2. WRONG FUNCTION NAME. The claim says use `agent.skill_utils.get_disabled_skills(config, platform)`. The actual function in `agent/skill_utils.py` is `get_disabled_skill_names(platform=None) -> Set[str]` (line 318) which takes NO config dict and reads from disk via `_load_raw_config()`. The function `get_disabled_skills(config, platform)` does exist — but in `hermes_cli/skills_config.py:27`, not in `agent.skill_utils`. Calling `agent.skill_utils.get_disabled_skills(config, platform)` will raise AttributeError. Security implication: if Script #2 imports from `agent.skill_utils` per the claim, the audit will crash and a user could be tempted to swallow the exception and fall back to raw file edits (bypassing cfg_get's platform-resolution indirection).

3. WRONG load_config/save_config SIGNATURE. The claim says `hermes_cli.config.load_config(path=str(profile_dir / "config.yaml"))` and `save_config(config, path=...)` work with a `path=` kwarg. The actual signatures at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/config.py:5266 and :5528 are `load_config()` and `save_config(config)` — NEITHER takes a `path` argument. Path resolution is hardwired via `get_config_path()` which reads `$HERMES_HOME` / `$HERMES_CONFIG` from the env. Security implication: passing `path=` will raise TypeError. The correct path-aware call is `hermes_constants.set_hermes_home_override(str(profile_dir))` (as profiles.py itself does in `_migrate_profile_config_if_outdated`, lines 472-481) — using a context-managed token. The claim's recommendation silently bypasses this safety net and would force callers to monkey-patch the env var or do raw YAML edits.

4. The `PLATFORMS` claim is also imprecise. `agent.skill_utils` does not export `PLATFORMS`; that lives in `hermes_cli/skills_config.py:23` (re-exported from `hermes_cli.platforms`). Iterating `PLATFORMS` from `agent.skill_utils` will fail.

5. The `do_install(..., skip_confirm=True, force=True, invalidate_cache=True)` claim about its call signature is correct, but the claim that `do_install` "internally uses `get_hermes_home()` which reads the env var, so direct in-process call already respects it" needs a caveat: at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/skills_hub.py:498 `do_install` calls `ensure_hub_dirs()` which writes to the GLOBAL `get_hermes_home()` from hermes_constants — not to the profile's HERMES_HOME. If `HERMES_HOME` is not set in the caller, install lands in ~/.hermes regardless of which profile you think you're targeting. Script #2 must set HERMES_HOME in os.environ BEFORE calling do_install (or use set_hermes_home_override) — the claim's "in-process call already respects it" is misleading.

6. Bundled manifest path claim is correct: /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/tools/skills_sync.py:41 has `MANIFEST_FILE = SKILLS_DIR / ".bundled_manifest"` and line 68 has `def _read_manifest() -> Dict[str, str]`. Confirmed.

7. HubLockFile + list_installed confirmed at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/skills_hub.py:595 (`lock = HubLockFile()`) and 906 (`hub_installed = {e["name"]: e for e in lock.list_installed()}`). Confirmed.

8. active_profile path confirmed at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/profiles.py:269-271: `return _get_default_hermes_home() / "active_profile"`. Confirmed.

9. `_get_default_hermes_home` (line 258) delegates to `hermes_constants.get_default_hermes_root()` which can be redirected in Docker/custom deployments — the claim's blanket "~/.hermes/" assumption is not always true.

10. clear_skills_system_prompt_cache(clear_snapshot=True) at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/prompt_builder.py:975 — confirmed; snapshot file is `.skills_prompt_snapshot.json` at line 971-972. Confirmed.
evidence:
Verified directly from the Hermes install at /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/:

- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/profiles.py:39-53 — actual _PROFILE_DIRS list (NO gateway/)
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/profiles.py:269-271 — _get_active_profile_path
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/profiles.py:472-481 — correct path-aware load pattern (set_hermes_home_override token)
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/agent/skill_utils.py:318 — get_disabled_skill_names (not get_disabled_skills)
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/skills_config.py:27 — get_disabled_skills(config, platform) actually lives here
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/config.py:5266, 5528 — load_config() and save_config(config) signatures (no path= kwarg)
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/skills_hub.py:478-498 — do_install signature + ensure_hub_dirs() env coupling
- /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/tools/skills_sync.py:41, 68 — bundled manifest

The architecture description is broadly right; the specific enumerated subdirectory list, the function names, and the load_config/save_config call signatures are wrong. Script #2 as specified in the claim would crash on the first call to `agent.skill_utils.get_disabled_skills(config, platform)` (AttributeError) and on `hermes_cli.config.load_config(path=...)` (TypeError). It would also miss the `gateway.pid` file because it would look in a non-existent `<profile>/gateway/` subdirectory.

verdict: Refuted. The Task E patch surface in /Users/kiscsicska/projects/easter-skills-hack-to-hermes-2/docs/maybe-patch-points.md enumerates exactly 4 files, not 5. agent/system_prompt.py is a re-exporter of MEMORY_GUIDANCE and SKILLS_GUIDANCE from agent/prompt_builder.py (see system_prompt.py lines 34/38 importing them) and contains no canonical definition of any Task-E prompt anchor. The Task E spec at §1/§2/§3/§4 explicitly names: agent/prompt_builder.py (SKILLS_GUIDANCE, MEMORY_GUIDANCE, build_skills_system_prompt), agent/background_review.py (_SKILL_REVIEW_PROMPT, _COMBINED_REVIEW_PROMPT, spawn_background_review_thread), tools/skill_manager_tool.py (optional schema-description clarification only), and website/docs/user-guide/features/skills.md (Agent-Managed Skills section). The current skills.md has zero mentions of "skill-creator" in prose (only the install command `/skills install openai/skills/skill-creator --force` at line 845, which the spec explicitly forbids presenting as bundled/mandatory). System_prompt.py is therefore at most a downstream consumer, not a primary candidate, and including it as a fifth file in the "final candidate set" inflates the count to 5 files, not the 8 sites claimed. The arithmetic "8 sites across files" is also inconsistent with the spec, which lists 4 files total.
